- Blank stare, followed by rightward head version with right arm extension; later bilateral tonic-clonic movements. This sequence is consistently observed across multiple episodes, often progressing to generalized clonic jerks involving both arms and legs with rhythmic shaking. The patient remains unresponsive throughout these events. - During one episode, the patient was observed in a hospital bed holding a phone to the ear, appearing to speak, with no clear signs of seizure activity. - In another instance, while holding a phone, the patient exhibited head tilt to the left, right arm extension, and subsequent clonic jerks in the right arm. - The patient was also seen holding a toy guitar during seizure activity, with right arm extension followed by left arm flexion consistent with fencer posturing. The patient remained unresponsive and in bed throughout. - At another time, while holding a toy guitar and wearing a cap, the patient had head tilt to the left, right arm flexed, and left arm extended, with no significant movement changes noted. - One observation documented the patient wearing a bandage and arm brace, initially sitting up then lying back, with head turning slightly left before rightward version, right arm extending then flexing, and left arm remaining relaxed. The patient appeared unresponsive during this sequence. - A detailed account confirms the typical pattern: blank stare initiating the event, followed by rightward head version and right arm extension, progressing to bilateral clonic jerks with rhythmic shaking of limbs, and sustained unresponsiveness throughout the seizure.

Multiple episodes characterized by a blank stare, followed by rightward head version with right arm extension, and subsequent bilateral tonic-clonic movements, including tonic-clonic jerks. In one case, the patient was unresponsive with head still, no head version, and no arm movements noted. One episode involved leg extension—right followed by left—suggesting tonic stiffening in the lower limbs, without observed clonic jerks. No movements or postural changes were observed in other segments, with the patient remaining supine, eyes closed, and motionless throughout.

Head movements include slight leftward turn followed by brief rightward deviation, occasionally without associated limb involvement. Right arm extension is observed in multiple episodes, sometimes accompanied by left arm flexion, while at other times the contralateral arm remains relaxed. Patient remains unresponsive during all observed events. Supine positioning with eyes closed is maintained initially in all segments, with variable emergence of motor signs.

The patient exhibits a blank stare followed by rightward head version and right arm extension, with the right arm remaining extended throughout the sequence in some instances. The left arm remains relatively still initially, with no flexion or extension noted in several observations. In multiple segments, this focal onset is followed by a transition to bilateral tonic-clonic movements, characterized by rhythmic jerking of both arms and legs. This progression includes a brief period of tonic stiffening prior to the onset of clonic jerks in several reports. In one detailed observation, clonic jerks are primarily apparent in the right arm, showing rhythmic flexion and extension, before becoming generalized. In contrast, some segments describe periods of initial stillness with eyes closed and no movement observed. During these episodes, the head may slightly turn to the left, followed by a brief rightward head version, without any associated arm movement. Subsequently, in certain cases, the right arm extends outward, sometimes accompanied by tonic stiffening, but without further progression to clonic activity. One segment notes right arm extension followed by left arm flexion, with no additional changes in posture. Throughout all observed events, the patient remains unresponsive. The seizure semiology is consistent across multiple episodes, beginning with a blank stare and asymmetric motor features (rightward head version and right arm extension), evolving into bilateral tonic-clonic activity in the majority of cases, indicating secondary generalization. A subset of observations captures non-convulsive or abortive sequences with minimal motor output beyond initial posturing.
